blanks.sentence: A simple "sorry" may be enough after a small accident, but not after something _____.
blanks.evidence: "Apologising depends on what you've done, so 'sorry' is fine if you accidentally step on someone's toes... But what if you do something really serious..." (L18)

blanks.sentence: In Ghana, a journalist apologised by following _____.
blanks.evidence: "Afia decided to say sorry to the King, following strict traditional rules, that she dress in black and apologise kneeling down on her knees." (L37)

blanks.sentence: She had to dress in _____ and apologise while kneeling.
blanks.evidence: "Afia decided to say sorry to the King, following strict traditional rules, that she dress in black and apologise kneeling down on her knees." (L37)

blanks.sentence: She had to dress in black and apologise while _____.
blanks.evidence: "Afia decided to say sorry to the King, following strict traditional rules, that she dress in black and apologise kneeling down on her knees." (L37)

blanks.sentence: In Pakistan, people are generally described as _____.
blanks.evidence: "I would say that Pakistanis generally - they're not really expressive, and we're also very cheeky..." (L43)

blanks.sentence: They may be _____, using humour or playfulness rather than directness.
blanks.evidence: "I would say that Pakistanis generally - they're not really expressive, and we're also very cheeky... Journalist Ahsan Mahmood thinks apologising in Pakistan is different because people are not expressive..." (L43-L52)

blanks.sentence: Some people avoid being very _____ about an apology.
blanks.evidence: "We would slide an apology in the middle of a conversation, and then very quickly move on to a new topic so that we don't have to... be very upfront about it." (L43)
"In Russia, we also have a tradition called Forgiveness Sunday, which happens on the last Sunday before Easter." (L46)

blanks.sentence: During it, people apologise for any _____ they have caused.
blanks.evidence: "The tradition is that you call your family and friends and apologise for any sorrows you've caused." (L46)

blanks.sentence: In Korean, apologies are shaped by a complex _____.
blanks.evidence: "In Korea, the way of saying sorry varies depending on the relationship between the speaker and the listener, due to the complex honorific system in the language." (L49)

multipleChoice.question: What is the main idea of the discussion?
options: Serious apologies usually require legal or religious procedures.
The meaning of apology changes depending on language and culture.
Public figures apologise more formally than ordinary people.
Most countries use the same words but different body language.
multipleChoice.evidence: "How do apologies change from culture to culture? Well, that's what we'll be finding out... Afia's story shows the influence of culture on how people say sorry." (L24-L40)

multipleChoice.question: What did Afia Pokua do when apologising to the King of the Ashanti tribe?
options: She offered a gift and bowed in front of his family.
She apologised on television to the whole country.
She wore black and knelt at the palace.
She called relatives to express regret.
multipleChoice.evidence: "Recently in Ghana... she found herself at his palace apologising... Afia decided to say sorry to the King, following strict traditional rules, that she dress in black and apologise kneeling down on her knees." (L21-L37)

multipleChoice.question: How are Pakistanis generally characterised in the discussion?
options: As emotionally open but unwilling to forgive.
As direct when apologising to older people.
As formal because apologies are socially serious.
As less expressive and somewhat playful.
multipleChoice.evidence: "I would say that Pakistanis generally - they're not really expressive, and we're also very cheeky..." (L43)

multipleChoice.question: Why might some Pakistanis put an apology inside another conversation?
options: To avoid being too openly direct about the apology.
To make sure the listener remembers the apology.
To follow a strict traditional rule about respect.
To show that the offence was not serious at all.
multipleChoice.evidence: "We would slide an apology in the middle of a conversation, and then very quickly move on to a new topic so that we don't have to... be very upfront about it." (L43)

multipleChoice.question: What contrast is shown between Pakistan and Korea?
options: Pakistan uses public apology rituals, while Korea avoids them.
Pakistan relies on silence, while Korea uses only gestures.
Pakistanis may avoid directness, while Korean apologies vary by relationship.
Pakistan treats apologies as private, while Korea treats them as religious.
multipleChoice.evidence: "We would slide an apology in the middle of a conversation... so that we don't have to... be very upfront about it. In Korea, the way of saying sorry varies depending on the relationship between the speaker and the listener..." (L43-L49)

multipleChoice.question: What opinion does Ahsan imply about Pakistani apologies?
options: They are usually too emotional to be comfortable.
They often happen indirectly rather than openly.
They should follow a more formal language system.
They are less sincere than apologies in other countries.
multipleChoice.evidence: "What we generally do is we would slide an apology in the middle of a conversation, and then very quickly move on to a new topic so that we don't have to... be very upfront about it." (L43)

multipleChoice.question: In "this is used in formal situations", what does "this" refer to?
options: The act of bowing.
The phrase "Che song ham ni da".
The honorific system as a whole.
The relationship between speaker and listener.
multipleChoice.evidence: "In many cases we say, 'Che song ham ni da.' This is used in formal situations or with people we don't know well, and we often bow when saying, 'Che song ham ni da.'" (L49)

multipleChoice.question: What can be inferred about saying sorry in Korea?
options: Choosing the wrong level of formality could affect how respectful the apology sounds.
People apologise only to strangers or people they do not know well.
Bowing is more important than the words used in the apology.
The same apology phrase is equally suitable in every situation.
multipleChoice.evidence: "In Korea, the way of saying sorry varies depending on the relationship between the speaker and the listener, due to the complex honorific system in the language... Honorific means showing respect and honour to someone." (L49-L61)

multipleChoice.question: Why is Afia Pokua's story used early in the discussion?
options: To show that apologising publicly is rare in journalism.
To introduce how culture can shape the form of an apology.
To argue that traditional apologies are always more sincere.
To explain why kings usually require strict apology rules.
multipleChoice.evidence: "Afia decided to say sorry to the King, following strict traditional rules... Afia's story shows the influence of culture on how people say sorry." (L37-L40)

multipleChoice.question: What is the overall implication of the examples?
options: A good apology depends only on choosing the correct phrase.
Gestures are replacing spoken apologies in many countries.
Apologies can involve social rules, relationships, and actions as well as words.
People apologise mainly because they fear public criticism.
multipleChoice.evidence: "Afia... following strict traditional rules... In Russia... you call your family and friends and apologise... In Korea, the way of saying sorry varies depending on the relationship..." (L37-L49)
